[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. (Any question received after then will not get an answer, and will more than likely just be deleted.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

The image on the left?  Another of the alphabetic wheels from Giordano Bruno's On the Shadows of the Ideas, showing the thirty letters he used for his system of mnemonics and conceptual algebra. The business in the center is made up of the five vowels AEIOU, because you can combine each letter in the wheel with one of the vowels: AA, AE, AI, AO, AU, BA, BE, and so on. 

[IMAGE:1]

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button above to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here.

comments.content: What are your thoughts and feelings about the theory and practice behind cartomancy, in particular with regular playing cards? How effective do you feel that they are in their use for divination and prediction of future events? I feel that it is best suited towards reading a person's psychological characteristics, and I have developed my own spread for that, which works very well, for some odd reason that I haven't been able to figure out.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: My take has always been that divination is a talent of diviners, not of their tools, though people often have an easier time with some sets of tools than with others. Thus it's not a matter of whether cards foretell the future, it's a matter of whether you as a diviner foretell the future -- and if ordinary playing cards help you do that, you're part of a grand old tradition. Ordinary playing cards were being used for divination long before anybody started using the tarot for that.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Could you elaborate a little more on that?  It had occurred to me that divination seemed less like a method of telling the future and more like a set of tools to help one see future possibilities and courses of action.  I'm not sure if I'm using the right word here but it seems more like harnessing one's one innate intuition (perhaps with divine guidance) to see a problem or situation more clearly.  But then I've misunderstood a lot of the occult so far.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's exactly what it's doing, and your intuition -- not the divinatory equipment -- is what's doing the work. Sometimes past and present actions have set things in motion in a particular direction so forcefully that the future is already set, and that's why diviners can sometimes foretell the future, sometimes in fine detail. In other situations you have choices -- but by and large, the more people are involved, the less choice you have.

comments.title: The Watcher
comments.content:
I think I've run across the Watcher.

I've been practicing the Heathen Middle Pillar for about two weeks now. A thought occurred to me in meditation that's been bothering me: the sword Sumarbrandr is used as the artifact of fire, which makes sense if it is indeed the sword used by Surtr, but then that association is bothersome to me.

I recognize that it was Frey's sword initially, but it bothers me nonetheless. I think I'm having trouble internalizing the notion that mythical time is not linear -- another thought that came up in meditation -- so you can't say Frey used to have it and now doesn't.

Anyway, last night, when performing the LBRP, I saw in my mind's eye Surtr to my right (not standing in the room, exactly, but it's hard to describe in what sense I saw him), and this persisted through the Middle Pillar. Then when I was doing the final Hammer Sign, as I extended my arms, I saw him reaching out to grab my right arm, and retracted it in fear, finishing the rite with my arm retracted.

(a) Was this the Watcher, or something more sinister?

(b) Given that there are other parts of the system that have needed to be changed, are my anxieties around Sumarbrandr pointing to a real concern, or just me being silly?

replies.title: Re: The Watcher

You've mentioned before that a lot of people who have past life memories of having been historical figures are allowing their egos to get in the way of reality, but I'd like to know how to tell if you may actually have been a significant historical figure. I'll start out by saying that it's not one that most people have heard of; in point of fact I was rather spooked when I starting to look into it and found out that yes, a person who did what my past life memories were talking about did exist. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
To my mind, the best test is to watch how you live this life. If you were a significant historical figure because you had certain virtues or strengths, those virtues or strengths should be evident in your behavior here and now. It's not too hard to see when somebody's confabulating glorious past lives to make up for being a complete loser here and now -- the instant question that comes to mind is "so how did you lose all your talents and charisma?"

I tend to believe that Gen. George Patton was telling the truth when he said that he had been a general in many past lives, because he acted like someone who'd been a general in many past lives. I had an experience once I've been meaning to ask about for a while, and this seems like a good opportunity.

I was reading the biography of a famous 20th century author, and I felt chills going up my spine as I recognized a mind that was more or less identical to my own. His interests, his likes and dislikes, the way he framed and thought about things, even his obvious omissions and justifications-- it was like I was looking into my own mind.

I was aware, of course, that people commonly believe themselves to be reincarnations of famous people, so I took it with a large helping of salt. But I've also noticed that much of my life path since starting magical training, and especially a lot of the obvious karma that I've faced, seems to fulfill certain missing pieces, missed opportunities and unfulfilled desires from this author's life.

Eventually I did a geomancy chart on the question, "Am I the reincarnation of X?" ...And I've lost the chart, but I remember that Llawenydd was the Right Witness, Pen y Ddraig was the Left Witness, and Via was the Judge. That seemed... suggestive. But I've wondered what you would make of it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Well, do you have literary talent, and are you a success in your chosen field?  The comments I made above about George Patton apply more generally -- it takes enthusiasm for hard work, as well as discipline and talent, to become a famous and successful writer, and those carry over from life to life. If you have them, it's possible -- but it's also worth remembering that there is a finite (though very large) number of personality types, especially within a shared culture. You may simply be the same type as the author.

replies.content:
jprussell,

This is a reply to your question from last week, about dice for rolling 1-25 for “pocket Ogham” throws. (I didn’t see the question until Tuesday.)

You can get “d5’s” off the shelf in several different forms. The most conventional is a standard d10 shape (pentagonal trapezohedron), but numbered 1-5 twice instead of 1-10. (Search for “d10 1-5 twice.”) While the trapezohedron is not a regular polyhedron (because the sides aren’t regular polygons), it’s “fair” in the sense of all the faces being the same shape and having the same configuration of adjacent sides and edge angles. There are other kinds of “d5” but not all of them are fair.

To roll 1-25, you roll two of them of different colors, designating one the “fives” and one the units. Read a 5 on the “fives” die as a zero. The roll result is 5 times the “fives” roll plus the units roll. That gives 1-25 with an even expected distribution.

A d10 numbered 0-4 twice (or 0-20 by fives, twice) would be more convenient for the fives die, but I don’t think those are available off the shelf.

What would be really ideal would be a custom set. Imagine a pair of d10, one of them marked with B, H, M, and A fews (feathered for directionality to distinguish between the first two), and blank, twice each. The other is marked twice on each face: with 1-5 strokes, and with one of the fews of the fifth aicme. (Also twice each.) You read the result directly by completing the aicme form given by the first die with the number of strokes given by the second die, unless the first die is blank, in which case the result is the fifth-aicme few on the second die.

Custom printed dice nowadays are not difficult to order, and some kinds can even be ordered in single quantities (but cost less per die if you can sell some copies). Minimum orders are larger if you need the figures molded in (embossed on each face) instead of printed on the surface.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thanks very much for this, Walt!

I knew that there were some "non-standard" dice configurations out there (mostly due to passing familiarity with DCCRPG, which enthusiastically employs Zocchi dice), but I didn't know specifically about the "d10 1-5 twice" configuration, which sounds like it might be helpful.

An alternate way I can imagine to read the two "d5s" is to treat one as the aicme and one as the place of the few, so 3, 5 would be 3rd aicme, 5th few, Ruis. Am I missing an obvious probability catch here that I should have noticed?

An added bonus there is that instead of numbers, if you went with custom dice, you could print elemental symbols to help reinforce "Earth of Water" and the like.

One advantage to the system you mention with a die for few form and a die for number of strokes is that it makes it easy to do a single roll with multiple fews - have a pair in a different color for each "draw". So, for example, you could have a yellow, green, and red pair and do a "Knowledge, Peace, Power" spread.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
There's no probability catch in reading the numbers the way you suggest. Your alternate way is essentially the same as my customized version, but done with numbers rather than symbols. Instead of multiplying and adding, then turning that number 1-25 back into a few based on a serial numbering of all the fews, you're recalling or mentally reconstructing the few by its place in the underlying 5x5 array.

It occurred to me that given you need two copies of each side of each die to identify a few using d10s, you could make use of that as well. For instance one set of the numbers or symbols on one die could be marked to indicate "inverted." The other die could be similarly marked to indicate... something else, perhaps whether the interpretation should lean toward self or other.

(If using pre-numbered dice, rather than go to the trouble of getting d10 numbered 1-5 twice, and then marking half the sides to distingish them, it might make more sense to use basic d10s and read 6-10 as "marked" 1-5. Depending on how easy you find it to quickly associate e.g. 8 with 3.)

(That reminds me of the early days of D&D, when all d20s were numbered 0-9 twice to facilitate use as percentile dice, and you had to mark half the sides to indicate high or low when you were rolling for a 1-20 result, which was the most common die roll in the game. And all the polyhedral dice were made of plastic so soft that whatever you used to mark the d20s always wore off, and the dice themselves eventually wore down into spheres. Then after the game you had to walk ten miles uphill in a snowstorm to get home.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Ah, yes, reversed fews! Well, that simplifies things somewhat. Here's a proposed procedure that should be do-able with a standard set of D&D dice:

1. Select a pair of d10s, one percentage and one regular, for each few you plan to "draw"
2. Roll all dice together (or separately, if needed to tell them apart)
3. Read the percentage die for which aicme, treating 6 = 1, 7 = 2, 8 = 3, 9 = 4, and 0 = 5; read the regular die as the few, with the same correspondences for 6-0, but with the added characteristic of "reversed"
3a. ALTERNATE: If you find it easier, treat the dice as a regular d100/percentage roll and assign a 4% chance to each few, with odd digits as upright and even as reversed. Example: a roll of 25-28% would be the 7th few, Duir, with 26 and 28 reversed.
4. Interpret as you would a card or stick draw.

The alternate method would be easier to use with a look up table, but for me the first option seems easier to do in my head (YMMV, of course)

Also, though I was not around to experience those d20s, I'm familiar with their history - my preferred niche of a niche hobby is the "Old School Renaissance" (folks who prefer older versions of D&D, whether because they never stopped or for other reasons), and I read Jon Peterson's excellent Playing at the World (it's extremely thorough).

JMG,

I've been meditating on the DA Grade V triads, and am attempting to understand the distinction between the door of invocation and the door of evocation (Note to non-DA readers: These terms have specific meanings assigned to them in this course that may differ from other discussions.).

I've gotten to a point where I kind of have two possible interpretations, and I'm wondering if you could clear up the author's intent here.

The epistemic interpretation: These descriptions outline a general epistemology.
Evocation is how you see spiritual beings in general--you see their reflection in your Magic Mirror of the Cosmos. (And, possibly, how you see everything outside of yourself: A reflection in your own MMotC, rather than the thing in itself.)
Invocation is how you improve the quality of the thing you're viewing, by moving closer to the spiritual being in question, in order to make the reflection more faithful to the real thing.

The literal interpretation: Invocation and evocation are actually separate modes of interacting with spiritual beings.
Epistemology aside, something you see through invocation is as separate from you as other incarnate humans you see on a day to day basis, you've simply moved your consciousness close enough to another plane to see the inhabitants there. This experience is clearly different from imaginal experience.
Evocation, by contrast, involves seeing the reflection of something else in your MMotC. Because the MMotC reflects the entire cosmos, the thing you want to see is already there in some sense. Because the MMotC is also the canvas upon which you see your own imaginings, the experience of viewing the MMotC is essentially the same as imagination, but you can learn which parts reflect internal things and which reflect external things through practice and study.

I immediately assumed the epistemic interpretation, but now I'm leaning toward the literal interpretation. The fact that I worked intensively on Leibniz in college might have lead to certain biases on my part.

So,

1: Which, if either, interpretation is the text meant to convey?

2: Are the details and implications I listed correct for that interpretation?

3: If it's the literal interpretation, I'm wondering if evocation as so described is a 1-way process or a 2-way process. If you're viewing your own MMotC, does the external entity actually know you're doing it and interact with you back, or is it like imaging a conversation with your own mental image of a person?

replies.content:
Continuing with responses to your multi-part question:

I'm currently learning the Ogham and I use the spread recommended in the DH and DMH: three draws, one for past (Knowledge), one for present (Peace), and one for potential (Power). I tend to look for a throughline that makes sense to connect all three, but sometimes the past and potential seem linked without the intervening present, in which case I'll interpret the present as a commentary or influence on the path from past to potential.

In the past, I've used a three-rune spread for the Elder Futhark, where the three were "Urdh, Verdhandi, and Skuld". This is very close to "past, present, potential", but there's some nuance to the Germanic way of looking at the Norns that makes things interesting. For example, "Skuld" literally means something like "What is Owed/Obligated" or maybe even "Debt". Not exactly the usual interpretation of future/potential - there's an idea that it reflects what must follow from choices made and things done in the past and present.

comments.title: Geomancy houses
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

a geomancy question: seeing the archetypal currents stirring, I'm curious about the future population of my country. I want to ask the oracle if in 2025 my country would have less than 2/3s of its current population but I'm unsure about the houses.

My question accordingly:

1) What house governs countries and (federal) states?
2) What house would be the quesited (population reduction)? Could that be the 12th, restrictions and limitations?
3) Should I rotate the house according to the querent? So, if I were to ask about the population of my city in 2025, I suppose it would be 3rd house (towns and cities) and 12th house (restrictions and limitations). The latter, adjusting for the third house, would come out the 2nd. Is that correct?

Thank you for your help!

replies.title: Re: Geomancy houses
replies.content:
You're venturing into what, as far as I know, is new territory, so there's going to be some guesswork involved. There is no single house for a nation -- as in mundane astrology, each house represents some part of the national whole. The 1st house represents the population; the 4th represents the land itself; the 10th represents the government, and so on. Death is an 8th house matter and sickness is a 6th house matter; 12th house is worth watching as a source of hidden dangers. The mundane geomancy charts I've read in Renaissance writings all took that direction, and didn't use modes of perfection -- they simply cast a chart and read the figures in each house.

If you want to use modes of perfection, I'd suggest using the 1st as the house of the querent (the population of the country) and 8th as the house of the quesited (death and pestilence), and see if you get a mode of perfection. Also read very carefully the figures in both houses and any other houses to which they spring.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy houses
replies.content: Ah, I didn't think it would be that innovative! I'll try some short-term questions first to get a feel for mundane geomancy...

replies.title: Re: Geomancy houses
replies.content: Keep detailed notes. You may want to publish a book or an article on the subject someday.

comments.title: Celebration of the rainy season
comments.content:
Hello, again.
I put this question in a different reply since it's a completely different topic.

We want to have a party in the garden I am working with. People who come to the garden are usually sensitive people, they love Nature and care for other people. They are not hard workers, though. We all have a Catholic background, and I am sure all of the spanish people here have renegated our religion. I am not so sure about the latin american people, I suspect some of them might still be faithful catholics, but there are very few of them.

We need to make a party. There have passed too many sad days with just work, little results and no celebrations, partly because of covid restrictions, partly because we were left out without water. I thought that a nice topic is the celebration of the beginning of our rainy season: That's the time when we can start planting and seeding again.

Now, my goal for this celebration is to strengthen the links in our little community and to create a sense of hope and purpose in our dryland gardening project. Maybe to communicate our good will to the spirit of the place. I can't use christian imagery (nor I want to), and even pagan imagery might be too much for some people. I think setting things as a game might not offense anyone. Of course, we would explain what we want to achieve to the participants so none is forced to participate.

I am not a party man, I don't know how to encourage people to break their shyness and I even usually refuse to do things that I feel are ridicule. So, any ritual I might do has to be meaningful and not ridicule, but at the same time it must be perceived as a game... it sounds hard.

I've been suggested to use drums, to write wishes in small papers and place them in an open trunk of an old olive tree. We also want to eat something just after the performance. Other people suggested to bring umbrellas and do something with them. We have also found a couple of rain related songs, but not the kind you can sing in choir.

comments.title: Selling your soul
comments.content: Many years ago, as a macho joke during a game of cards, I bid my soul on a hand and lost. The winner, one upping me, immediately said that he had exchanged it for a single night with a succubus. This was all 'in jest' (or maybe tempting fate), but the superstitious part of me can't help wondering if that sort of teenage mistake might have actually happened. replies.title: Re: Selling your soul
replies.content:
1) No, a joke won't do it, though it may attract the attention of malign spirits. Any form of religious or spiritual practice should fix any lingering effects.

2) The soul is a little hard to describe if you don't have a background in occult philosophy. Basically, it's the part of you that accumulates knowledge and character from life to life. Your spirit is eternal; your body, of course, is very temporary; your soul is the structure of consciousness created by your life experience in many lives, and it passes from life to life, growing and changing as you go. It's the basis for the personality you evolve in each life, and each personality adds to it. To sell your soul is to place that structure under the power of an evil spirit, to do with as it wishes, as long as it wishes. You're basically setting yourself up to be a chew toy between lives -- and it's quite common for death to come sooner and rebirth to come much, much later than it otherwise would. That is to say, this is not a good idea...

You wrote: "I tend to believe that it was largely sailors, Polynesian mainly, that actually settled this land."

That is certainly my sense of the land's history. In the long, long history of the lands around the Mediterranean Sea, the easiest and most heavily used migration routes were pretty clearly by sea rather than overland. If you're migrating overland, mountains and rivers are enormous obstacles. Though the risks are greater, the obstacles are much less formidable if you're traveling a long distance by sea.

The same is well documented for Medieval Sandinavia, where boat-building was one of the finest of fine arts. Also, "experimentalists" like Thor Heyerdahl have proven beyond any reasonable doubt that transoceanic travel has always been not just possible, but a quite reasonable option.

It's just academic archaeologists who are shy of positing transoceanic migration. They seem to have no sense of the profound difference between risks and obstacles; and also almost all career academics have been trained to be risk-adverse. High mountains are obstacles; lethal storms at sea are risks. They are two entirely different things.

Bear in mind that spirits were here in the land well before any humans came to live there--indeed, aeons before the very first humans ever walked the earth. They are immeasurably older than our whole species is.

The humans who first settled any part of the land have had barely enough time to interact with just some of those spirits, not with all of them--not even nearly with all of them, so numerous are they. (That is true even where humans have been there for millenia.) So indeed some spirits, having entered into alliance with some aboriginal tribes, may be hostile to us late-comers, since we conquered and (nearly) exterminated the former inhabitants with whom they had established a firm relationship. Tread warily!

There will be countless other spirits, however, who have no such grounds for hostility. You mightt be able to forge a relationship with some of them, if and only if they see any small advantage for themselves in doing so. Many of them will not see any such advantage. After all, as a species we humans are such raw newcomers on their land, and the spirit world as a whole can have no universal ancient traditions of interaction with our species. The spirits do not really need us, by and large; it is we who seem to need them.

You may be lucky and find one or a few spirits who might interact with you, although they have no compelling need to do so. But at least some of those spirits will take such a relationship as a life-long serious obligation on your part. If you default on it even ten or twenty years down the road, they may punish you, or even try to kill you. (Jordan Paper had a very instructive account of such a mistake that he made early in his life, when he started to interact with a spirit of one small part of the land, which nearly cost him his life. See his The Deities are Many (2005).)

So tread warily here, too.

Hi JMG,

Particularly in Southern conjure magic, crossroads come up a lot, such as using crossroads dirt in hoodoo workings.

In addition, several blues musicians are said to have summoned the devil at some crossroads to sell their souls for whatever purpose.

And finally, gallows were often sited at crossroads.

comments.title: Orthodoxy, Sophia
comments.content:
This might be more suited for Robert Mathiesen.

As far as I understand, many ideas associated with, say, Solovyov, are really influenced by German Romanticism and Western esotericism ("Martinism" and so on).

But what about Sophia and Sophiology? Is this also a thing Solovyov and Bulgakov got from Western esoteric sources, or is it based on authentic Russian Orthodox traditions, or perhaps Russian folk spirituality?

Bulgakov (or at least his Sophiology) was apparently condemned by a exile Russian Orthodox synod as heresy, but doesn´t veneration of Sophia exist generally within the Orthodox Church?

Tidlösa, Sweden

replies.title: Re: Orthodoxy, Sophia
replies.content:
Sophia has been venerated as a saint in the Orthodox Church forever. The Great Church in Constantinople (now Istanbul) was dedicated to her before it was forcibly converted into a mosque. In Greek, her name means "wisdom"; and she is the mother of a triad of saints named Faith (Pistis), Hope (Elpis) and Love (Agape). She and her daughters are often depicted together on a single icon. In every Divine Liturgy, right before the Gospel is read, the priest intones "Wisdom! Attend!" where the word "Wisdom" translates "Sophia" in the original Greek text. So there are layers upon layers here, which I (who am not Orthodox) barely grasp.

No doubt the condemnation of Bulgakov and Solovyov's sophiogy was due to their ecclesticism, which blended ancient Orthodoxy with Western currents of esoteric thought.

replies.title: Re: Orthodoxy, Sophia
replies.content:
I wouldn't interpret "Sophia, orthi!" as an invocation of Wisdom as a person, though. It's kind of a "hey, here comes the Gospel, pay attention!" moment.

It is true that there are icons depicting holy Wisdom and her daughters faith, hope, and love. Some people maintain that they were actual people, but mostly they are regarded as metaphorical.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I am reading a book by Jacob E. Van Vleet entitled Informal Logical Fallacies as a way to learn them more fluently. However, reading the book has made it apparent to me that it is very easy to disguise arguments in a supposedly objective form, especially if you happen to be a materialist (like it appears the author is).

A few of the fallacies conflict with things I am finding when reading occult philosophy. For example, he catalogs the Subjectivist Fallacy, which usually takes the form "That may be true for you, but it is not true for me." He writes later in the entry that we "must distinguish between belief and truth."

Yet in reading Jung, he points out that many phenomena are dependent on the subject/observer relationship, that the nature of the relationship between a specific therapist and a specific patient is a key factor in the healing or individuation process.

Another example: what Van Fleet calls the Anthropomorphism Fallacy, which "occurs when one assumes a non-human being or thing has the same characteristic as a human". One of the examples he cites: "Because of our lack of respect for nature, the earth has become angry with us humans." I understand his point, that doing this could be a form of projection, but again in occult philosophy, if everything has a form of consciousness in it, and there are indeed spirits of places, plants and indeed the planet, then this could indeed be what is at work. Maybe not in as simple a form as human anger.

A similar issue exists with his entry about the Fallacy of Composition. His examples for this make sense and are useful. But one of his examples betray this materialist bias: "Some life forms exhibit signs of sentience. Therefore, all life forms must be sentient." In a way, it could be that it is actually a simpler answer, more realistic answer to the problem of consciousness to assume that everything does have a form of sentience.

One last example: he includes an entry called Appeal to Tradition ("appeals to tradition as justification for its conclusion"). Yes, but he also fails to include a fallacy which I could entitle Appeal to Futurism ("appeals to future possibility as justification for its conclusion").

I'm still reading the book, but I am waiting for an example of this type of logic: "Because there is no evidence for this, it is therefore untrue," to which my counter is "Just because there is no evidence for this, it also does not mean that isn't true. It just means there is currently no evidence."

I'm interested in this conflict between the type of thinking that is useful in an occult practice, and the border between that and the type of logic outlined in that book and others. It's almost like logic can work, but it is surrounded by a murkier area where perhaps the rules of logic don't really apply.

I'm finding that reading the book, and seeing what I perceive to be the limits of logical thinking is a little alarming in one way, but also is an example for me about how subjective and mythic thinking seems to shape what we call logical thinking.

Not really a question, just more interested in you had any comments in response. Many thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That kind of thing is extremely common. Rationalists have their own unquestioned dogmas and their own version of blind faith, and in the usual human fashion, these keep them from seeing their own logical blunders.

comments.title: Phil Parker's Lightning Process (LP)
comments.content:
In some European countries (don't know about the USA), there's controversy around the so-called "Lightning Process", developed by British osteopath Phil Parker. Particularly controversial is the fact that some people diagnosed with chronic fatigue syndrome or ME have reported dramatic improvements, even miraculous remissions, after attending three-day LP trainings. (Others have the opposite experience, having their condition worsen after the course; yet others report improvements followed by relapses.) Perhaps it wouldn't be so controversial if the training courses didn't cost hundreds of dollars to attend, and if the techniques taught were openly available and not kept quasi-secret as a proprietary, trademarked "process".

I'm copy-pasting below what seems like a "leaked" description of the core LP technique. Is it just me, or does this look like a commercially wrapped magical ritual? Potentially powerful and useful in some circumstances, possibly harmful in others? Perhaps best used with care and/or as part of a broader practice?

A specific question for our gracious host: Does this look like something else in particular? Like, is there reason to believe that Dr Parker himself "copy-pasted" it from another source?

Source: http://miriampotter.blogspot.com/2012/08/the-lightning-process-revealed-for-all.html

* Every time you have a remotely negative thought or a thought that relates to being ill, you have to say to yourself:

1. STOP! (and do a stop like action. In therapy this actually involves writing 'Stop' on a piece of paper, putting it on the floor, and jumping on it to reinforce the action)

2. Do I want to be sick or do I want a life I love?

3. I want a life I love.

4. Well done (this action of rewarding yourself is very important)

5. What do you want?

6. I want to feel/be ....... (must be something positive that relates to how well you want to feel)

7. How are you going to do that?

8. I'm going to visualize a time when I felt like this (there is always a time somewhere in your memory when you can remember how good it felt to be well and feel well. Make this visualization as vivid as possible by imagining the smells, sights, sounds, etc. Do this with your eyes closed and re-live the memory vividly. Use several different memories if you can.)

* If you have a negative thought at any time during this process, you must stop and start again.

* Repeat this process over and over as often as possible for any negative thought that comes to mind.

replies.title: Re: Phil Parker's Lightning Process (LP)
replies.content: Okay, what Parker is doing is a standard New Thought process, rebranded and marketed to a new audience. Yes, it will work for some people, give temporary benefits to others, and do nothing for a third group -- that's typical for New Thought healing techniques. Yes, it would be better done in the context of some more extensive body of training and practice!

comments.title: Folk Magic v. Natural Magic
comments.content:
What is the difference between folk magick and natural magick. Could you explain each. Are does folk magick just have a lot of overlap with natural magick or are they the same thing completely?
Thank you, E.L.

replies.title: Re: Folk Magic v. Natural Magic
replies.content:
Okay, folk magic is magic practiced by the common folk, as distinct from the magic of the educated. It includes natural and ceremonial magic, but uses relatively simple forms of each. Natural magic is any form of magic that gets its power from magical energies in natural things -- herbs, stones, resins, and so on. If the power comes from a natural object or substance, it's natural magic.

For example, if you make a protective amulet by filling a little cloth bag with protective herbs, that's natural magic. If you make a protective amulet by taking something that doesn't have protective properties and performing a ritual to make it protective, that's ceremonial magic.

If you're working class and you learned how to make the amulet from your grandmother, it's folk magic. If you went to university and learned how to make the amulet from a book by an occult writer like me, it's not!

comments.title: Spiritual Alchemy: Conscious and Unconscious Material
comments.content:
Hi JMG

Thank you for doing Magic Mondays.

I am one of the people doing the Spiritual Alchemy course. In that regard I have a question, but seeing as it isn't a question of how to do the course or any issues with the course itself I thought it would fit better within the Magic Monday context.

From what I have seen of the Spiritual Alchemy course so far, and seeing as all of it isn't public yet, I don't know if this observation is entirely accurate when looking at the order it in its entirety.

Anyhow, as for my understanding and the reason for the question: The work of the order concerns primarily our collected emotional and mental material. And by observing and working with memories, thoughts, views, beliefs and so forth we have an opportunity to start processing them - leading to a different outcome. Turning lead to gold as it were.

Now, if we contrast that with the work and insights of certain psychologist/psychoanalysts of the past 100 years, there is a counter point to be made that some/much of our Prima Materia isn't readily available to our conscious mind. We may indeed have the best intention of working through that which we have access to, but it may be somewhat limited. Some of this relates to how emotions and other things are 'stored' in the body.

In particular I am thinking of the work of Reich here (but others come to mind too). He would have been allergic to the use alchemical language in relation to his work, which is an amusing irony.

Some related questions to this: is more unconscious material made conscious through the process of spiritual alchemy? would certain types of working with the body (bodywork) be a helpful adjunct to the work of spiritual alchemy?

Now, I understand that I am asking a very broad question here, and that there is neither time, space or scope for a full answer here, but if you could point in the general direction of material that you think would be helpful that would also be appreciated. Conversely, if it is asking too much, or something that you aren't prepared or willing to answer while you are putting up the course I certainly understand that too.

Thank you again for putting out the course.


- Mint Fragrant Ouroboros

replies.title: Re: Spiritual Alchemy: Conscious and Unconscious Material
replies.content:
The Order of Spiritual Alchemy isn't a system of depth psychology. It works with what's conscious, or more precisely with what's accessible to consciousness -- most people find that material they had forgotten or pushed aside surfaces in fair quantity while doing the work. Depth psychology is one approach, this is another, and yes, they approach the question of personal change using different methods and with different presuppositions.

My take, having read extensively in both schools, is that depth psychology is more useful if you've got a conversion disorder or any other neurosis based on repression. If you don't -- if your emotional issues are a matter of past experiences that are poorly integrated rather than actively repressed -- consciousness-based methods such as the one offered by the OSA are at least as effective -- and also much less expensive. Still, your mileage may vary.

replies.content:
Many thanks for this.

- Mint Fragrant Ouroboros

comments.content:
Hello JMG -

In an earlier post, you quoted the following from Dion Fortune:

"American occultism will never come into its own until it ceases to import its systems from Europe and India, but goes back along the lines of its own tradition, picking up the aboriginal contacts, and daring to bend them to its own evolutionary purposes. It must seek the contacts of the Sun Temple of Atlantis through the Maya tradition. Egypt has no message for the United States. Americans can learn esoteric philosophy and science from the Western tradition, just as Europeans can learn them from the Eastern tradition, but the initiatory forces cannot be conveyed across the Atlantic or the Pacific. Some day there will come an American who will pick up the ancient Maya contacts, adapt them to modern needs, and express them in an initiatory ritual which shall be valid for the civilization to which he belongs."

This got me thinking. I’m an American citizen, and was born here, but my family, by blood, is almost entirely English, though we’ve been on this continent since the Mayflower. I’ve chosen Thelema as my path for a number of reasons, but a chief one of those is said almost entirely English heritage. Crowley’s system is syncretic of both Western and Eastern traditions, sure, but its magical roots are in the Golden Dawn tradition and practically speaking it seems the symbol sets and ritual work draw from the Western elements of Thelema over the Eastern. As such, I feel like my subconscious responds very well to not just the symbolism and ritual but also Crowley’s (and, for that matter, Mathers and Westcott’s) mode of expression. So reading Fortune’s quote I was quite surprised to see how much she seems to be saying is staked on whichever particular land mass a magician calls home.

So I suppose my question is - in terms of a magician’s capacity to connect to the system they’re practicing, how much is country of residence a factor and how much is heritage? Does one tend to matter more than the other?

I hope this makes sense. I’m not certain my question is all the way cooked yet, but I want to get it in before Monday ends.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The English-speaking countries of North America are in a transitional state just now, and it's a transition that will take centuries to complete. Fortune was speaking from the long view, with an eye toward the future civilization that will arise here after 2600 e.v. or so. That's why she says "some day." For now, those of us who live here have a foot in both worlds, partly attuned to the Faustian-European current and partly attuned to something that has not yet been born. You practice Thelema, which was brought here quite early after its founding and very quickly established a presence; I practice Druidry, which has the same history -- the first Druid organization in North America was founded in upstate New York in the 1790s. For our time, and for some generations to come, that's going to be a valid approach, and by the time the future American civilization gets under way, if either of our traditions has survived, it will have acclimatized.

comments.title: Is magical practice wise to take on at this time in my life?
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

I’ve been reading your blogs since ADR. I could very easily gush nonstop over how much your writing and teachings have impacted me and my thinking but then this post would be unbearably longer than it already is. I’ll just say for now that you have my respect and gratitude, as does this community here.

I’m writing for the first time today to ask if it's safe to consider taking on learning to practice magic at this moment in my life. To my surprise over the last few years I’ve found myself more and more drawn to your writing on magic. After a particularly dramatic shift in my life earlier this year, I decided to learn the SoP and begin practicing daily. Here’s what happened in short order -

After about 4 weeks in January of learning and practicing the SoP per your instructional posts, my relationship of 9 years, which had recently gone through a physical separation while we maintained contact to continue working on things, completely blew up due a surprising, unexpected spontaneous action on my part. This led to a period of complete cessation of contact between us for about a month. Then, less than a week after this split, I was contacted out of the blue for a freelance project in my previous field that offered way more money with a high profile client than the factory work I was doing at the time (work which I took on after an extended period of unemployment following the death of a parent and subsequent heavy family issues that triggered a period in my life of just drifting, in an effort to break my inertia and ease myself back into the world of work). I took the freelance job, which turned out to be an ice cold splash of stress to the face after a long break from this work (digital media production). My partner and I then met at an agreed-upon date to see where we both were. I wanted to reconcile and try again, she told me she needed another 6 weeks of separation as she traveled out of state to sort out her feelings. During those 6 weeks, within the month of April, my mental state began to seriously deteriorate to the point of daily panic attacks and a near nervous breakdown. I ended up seeing a psychiatrist and went on medication, which I likely have to credit with pulling me out of the abyss. Around this time I read a post on Magic Monday where you advised someone who was taking anti-psychotic meds that it would be prudent to avoid magical practice as their psyche was likely not up for it. While I wasn’t psychotic, one of the medications I was taking was technically an antipsychotic so I decided to stop the SoP for the time being as I allowed my mental state to stabilize. When my partner and I reunited, she had decided she wanted to end the relationship. As painful as this was for me to hear, I understood it and on some level knew that it was healthier for me as well.

Over the following weeks, the freelance project continued to get more stressful and I struggled to come to terms with my new reality - alone with next to no social support in one of the most expensive areas in the country, only now starting to scrape together an income, in an unpleasant living situation because it was all I could afford, and feeling utterly lost. While I no longer suffered daily panic attacks, I was still struggling emotionally.

Fast forward to now. I completed the project and for the past few weeks have been researching my next job move. I’m no longer taking the antipsychotic but still taking two lower-level medications for anxiety. I’m grieving the loss of the relationship heavily, along with many of my choices and actions over the last number of years that have led me to this point. Grieving, but not in a depression, which I now realize I’d been in for several years prior to these events. I find myself at a turning point where I’m waking up to lifelong habits of self-sabotaging thinking, behavior and emotional reactivity, and I have the awareness and energy to make changes. It feels like a huge undertaking - I’m getting to know myself, my values and goals in this life, and a sense of agency for maybe the first time now in my late forties. I still have moments of anxiety over my uncertain future and deep sadness (fits of sobbing are a daily occurrence), but I’m not in crisis. I’m working with someone who’s technically a therapist but is really more of a mentor and spiritual guide with a Buddhist bend. I am exercising regularly and have begun making new social connections. I am easing into the OSA work you’ve been posting. I should also note that I’m constantly aware of synchronicities happening in my life all the time.

I’ve been interested in magical practice for the self-improvement benefits I’ve read and heard you talk about so many times, and to align myself with the forces of life and the universe so that I may work together with them, call on them for support and feel connected to something greater than myself. I was raised Catholic and while I don’t consider myself a current devotee I am working at re-establishing my relationship with Jesus, invoking Him in the SoP and occasionally praying again. I’m not sure where I stand on His existence and influence on my life at the moment, but I am open to receiving what contact and experience comes. So now that you have all of this context, I’d like to ask - is magical practice something that would support all the changes and efforts that I’m making in my life right now, or would I do well to let some of the dust settle before taking anything like this on?

Thank you for reading and I welcome your thoughts and wisdom.

Once you've sorted things out on the material plane, then magical practice might be a suitable choice for you.

replies.content: Thank you very much for your response. I suspected now might not be a good time to get into magical practice, but I'll look forward to digging into the OSA course. I'm finding it to be as much of a challenge as you suggested it would.

comments.title: Golden Dawn
comments.content:
Thank you, as always, for your work JMG. I have three very different questions (hopefully this isn't too much to ask of you):
1) Perhaps more of a druid question - do you believe that souls have some choice over their parents and when they incarnate?
2) I've been practicing the LMP for about a week now - so not long at all - but I've already felt it's effects so powerfully. comments.title: SOP
comments.content: Hi JMG, I'm slowly working through learning the SOP ritual (currently learning the Air sequence), but wanted to ask about memorization. I have a bad memory, especially mid-performance (call it stage fright). Do you have any tips on committing ritual passages to memory? Is it natural that you feel a little clumsy when starting out or is that a sign you are underprepared? Thanks so much and have a great evening.

replies.title: Re: SOP
replies.content: Make a cheat sheet and have it with you to prompt your memory if you need it. Don't sweat it -- some people have better memories than others. With daily practice you'll get it down sooner or later.

replies.title: Re: SOP
replies.content:
As a bit of additional advice, if I may (I teach students public speaking, so I hope I can offer something useful): doing it daily will get it into your memory at some point. You can accelerate the process a bit if you try to recall the words without checking your cheat sheet immediately - if you just read along, it will take a bit longer for it to sink in. If you really want to get it into memory fast, you could make up some flashcards.

The other thing I have found has been helping me (I'm only up to the Fire gate!) is noticing the patterns in the sequences (this will be more help once you get past Air and see that the others work pretty much the same way) - "By [animal symbol] [placement of animal symbol], and in the great name [DEITY], I invoke the [Element], its Gods, its Spirits, its Powers. May I take on the blessings of [Element] this day. I thank the [Element] for its gifts."

Lastly, a bit of psychology: I've found it helpful to remind myself that doing the ritual poorly is better than not doing it at all, and that I'll get better by stumbling through it. If you're the kind of person that likes to get things right, that can be a bitter pill to swallow, but weirdly, relaxing about "doing it right" helps most people actually remember things they're trying to memorize, and stressing about getting it perfect makes it harder.

replies.title: Re: SOP
replies.content: Great tips thank you so much

comments.title: Thor and Infinity
comments.content:
So I had some thought about Thor and the Midgard Serpent in relation to Faustian culture (as I recall, the homeland of Faustian culture is in roughly the same area as the homeland of Germanic mythology).

Thor's great enemy is the Midgard Serpent, who he only succeeds in slaying at Ragnarok, when they kill each other. And slaying the Midgard Serpent was one of the three challenges Thor was famously unable to do, the others being defeating old age and drinking the ocean. Now, the Midgard Serpent lies around the earth, which suggests an association with limits. (In particular, he might represent the veil between us and the gods.) Old age is certainly a limit, and the ocean is often used to represent vastness as such.

It seems to me, then, that the mythology around what Thor cannot do speaks to the futility of the Faustian quest for the Infinite: if even the mightiest god cannot destroy the great limits and fully embrace the limitless -- at least without being destroyed by them in turn -- what hope do the rest of us have?

I realize these thoughts aren't really coming together coherently, but it seems like there's a connection there, I'm just not sure whether Thor was a warning or if he is the embodiment of the then-nascent Faustian culture, or both.

replies.title: Re: Thor and Infinity
replies.content: This seems perfectly coherent to me. Yes, I think you're on to something important.

replies.title: Re: Thor and Infinity
replies.content:
This sounds like a fruitful line of thought for meditation!

I think it is also significant in the Utgard-Loki story that even though Thor cannot defeat any of those three challenges, he makes enough of a dent in each to scare the hell out of Utgard-Loki - he gets one of Old Age's legs up, he creates the tides by downing a significant chunk of the ocean, and he shakes the world by partially lifting the Midgard Serpent. To combine this with your insight, it seems the myth is saying that yes, you can do pretty mighty things if you strive for it, but no, those aren't infinite in their scope.

In other words, I largely agree, but I think the myth well captures the ambivalence of the last few hundred years of Faustian culture - believing you can do anything will let you do a hell of a lot, but not actually anything. Which is to say that I think Thor's behavior going into Utgard is a warning (that maybe the culture at the time didn't consciously realize!), but perhaps there is wisdom there in both the end of the Utgard story and in Ragnarok - striving against limits has a certain constrained usefulness (ironically enough), and that the final defeat of limits is actually the destruction of the system that enabled the striving. It's possible that Thor killing the Midgard serpent in Ragnarok is ultimately "good" or "useful", but if so, only to the new world that can arise after they both are dead, and that new world will surely have new strivers and new embodiments of limits.

replies.title: Re: Thor and Infinity
replies.content: Thank you for this. I think you're right. And you're definitely right that this deserves some meditation.

comments.title: Divinity flows
comments.content:
If you are focusing on keeping yourself aligned with/in tune with your deity and are keeping it clean etc and doing whatever they ask of you then you would be a worshiper of god x.

What is the difference between being a worshiper of a deity and being a priest of that deity.

Is a mystic just a worshiper that goes a little deeper?

If you are focusing on being a channel of your god do you automatically start to exhibit their tendencies or does that require a concentrated focus (eg if your god was a healing god would you start shedding healing energies or would you need to want it badly and take steps to bring it to light)

If you were focusing on developing a pathway and your god didn't have that pathway as part of their portfolio does it not work, work badly, an allied deity would provide, you would need to add another god who does have it, an enemy would step in...?

(Of course just because you think something is a good idea to develop / have flow through you might not mean that the deity who chose you / you chose is obligated to help you on that journey.)

replies.title: Re: Divinity flows
replies.content:
The difference between a worshipper and a priest is that the priest helps other people worship. The difference between an ordinary worshipper and a mystic is that to the mystic, the relationship with the deity is the most important thing in life and takes precedence over everything else.

The other things depend on the deity and the nature of your relationship. Some healing deities will give you healing gifts without asking for them, others will make you work for it. Some gods have close relationships with other gods and some don't. It really varies.

comments.content: What, if any, are the implications of practicing a magical system without believing in its core philosophy? From my reading it seems most traditional systems have some degree of Axial age religious baggage: life as we know it is so inferior or unreal or whatever the goal of magic should be to dissolve yourself, exit this plane of existence, etc etc. This seems to recur across most eastern and western systems, particularly those with any basis in Christianity or the Dharmic religions. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
First, belief is overrated. Do you have to believe in gravity to toss something into the trash?

Second, that's the legacy of the Piscean age. Now that we're in the Aquarian age, traditions are shifting to accommodate the new celestial influence. You certainly don't have to buy into the Piscean ideology to practice, say, the Golden Dawn system -- William Butler Yeats was a GD Adeptus Exemptus and you'll have to look long and hard to find someone less interested in the Piscean take on things, just for one example.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: If I may, as someone who picked up a systematic magical practice as an atheist, the core implication of starting down a path without believing in the core philosophy is that sooner or later you will come to understand how and why that philosophy makes sense.
